<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
  - Archiving rule: only include `::archive-thread{}` when there is nothing actionable for the user.
  - Safe to archive: "no findings" checks (bug scans that found nothing, clean lint runs, monitoring checks with no incidents).
  - Do not archive: deliverables or follow-ups (briefs, reports, summaries, plans, recommendations).
  - If you do archive, include the archive directive after the inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.
</app-context>

payload.message:
That `.zip.lck` is a stale Gradle wrapper lock, not a PeeDom compile error. I checked it: there’s a matching `.zip.ok` next to it, which means the Gradle zip finished downloading and the lock file was just left behind. I also verified `:app:assembleDebug` and the Hermes JS bundle succeed from the terminal, so the project itself is building.

Run this from PowerShell:

```powershell
Stop-Process -Name studio64 -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
Remove-Item "C:\Program Files\Android\Android Studio\wrapper\dists\gradle-8.14.3-bin\cv11ve7ro1n3o1j4so8xd9n66\gradle-8.14.3-bin.zip.lck" -Force
cd C:\Users\T\Desktop\PeeDom
npm run android:studio
```

If that `Remove-Item` says access denied, use an elevated PowerShell once and run:

```powershell
Remove-Item "C:\Program Files\Android\Android Studio\wrapper\dists\gradle-8.14.3-bin\cv11ve7ro1n3o1j4so8xd9n66" -Recurse -Force
```

Then relaunch with:

```powershell
cd C:\Users\T\Desktop\PeeDom
npm run android:studio
```

If Studio still tries to use `C:\Program Files\Android\Android Studio\wrapper\dists\...`, set Gradle user home inside Studio to:

```text
C:\Users\T\.g\peedom
```

Path:
`Settings > Build, Execution, Deployment > Build Tools > Gradle > Gradle user home`

If you want, I can also patch the launcher script to be more aggressive about cleaning stale Studio wrapper locks.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.user_instructions:
# ~/.codex/AGENTS.md

## General Working Agreements
- **Zero Placeholder Policy:** Never use `// TODO`, `// implementation goes here`, or dummy data. If a function is called, it must be fully implemented with production-grade logic.
- **Defensive Programming:** Every async operation must include a `try/catch` block, a loading state, and a user-friendly error handler.
- **Strict Typing:** Use TypeScript for everything. Avoid `any` at all costs. Interfaces must be descriptive and exhaustive.
- **Performance First:** Use `React.memo`, `useMemo`, and `useCallback` strategically to prevent unnecessary re-renders in mobile environments.
- **Tooling:** Prefer `pnpm` for all dependency management. Always run `npm run lint` and `npm test` after modifying logic.
- **Security:** Never hardcode API keys or secrets. Always use `.env` files and validated environment variables.

--- project-doc ---

# Pee-Dom Agent Operating Contract

This repository is a mobile-first application named Pee-Dom.

## Product Scope
Pee-Dom is a restroom-finder mobile application for iOS and Android users. It is built for launch readiness, reliability, offline tolerance, and app-store compliant mobile UX.

## Platform Priorities
1. Mobile-first behavior
2. Expo / React Native compatibility
3. TypeScript correctness
4. Supabase schema and RLS correctness
5. Offline-first behavior
6. Safe-area, navigation, permissions, and degraded-network handling
7. Production debugging, telemetry readiness, and launch hardening

## Engineering Constraints
- Prefer React Native primitives, Expo-compatible solutions, and TypeScript-safe patterns
- Do not introduce web-only assumptions into mobile flows
- Protect auth/session, location access, and user-submitted data
- Any change touching data flow must consider:
  - offline queue behavior
  - optimistic updates
  - retry semantics
  - idempotency
  - RLS implications
- Any change touching navigation must consider:
  - Expo Router route behavior
  - back-stack behavior
  - deep-link viability
  - modal interaction
- Any change touching maps/location must consider:
  - permission denial states
  - stale location
  - background/foreground transitions
  - battery and performance costs

## Review Standard
All agent reviews should evaluate:
- correctness
- edge cases
- race conditions
- offline behavior
- mobile UX regressions
- security/privacy issues
- Supabase/RLS impact
- launch risk

## Output Standard
Agents must produce:
- explicit assumptions
- concrete file targets
- exact code changes or diffs
- risks
- test checklist

# PeeDom

Expo Router mobile app for finding bathrooms. Android local development is supported through the tracked native `android/` project, while release builds remain on EAS.

## Prerequisites

- Node.js 22.x
- Android Studio installed at `C:\Program Files\Android\Android Studio`
- Android SDK installed at `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Android\Sdk`

Set these user environment variables on Windows before opening Android Studio:

- `JAVA_HOME=C:\Program Files\Android\Android Studio\jbr`
- `ANDROID_HOME=%LOCALAPPDATA%\Android\Sdk`
- `ANDROID_SDK_ROOT=%LOCALAPPDATA%\Android\Sdk`
- `GRADLE_USER_HOME=%USERPROFILE%\.g\peedom`

Ensure your user `Path` includes:

- `%JAVA_HOME%\bin`
- `%ANDROID_HOME%\platform-tools`
- `%ANDROID_HOME%\emulator`

If PowerShell blocks `npm` or `npx` shim execution, use `npm.cmd` and `npx.cmd` instead.

## Android Studio workflow

1. Install dependencies with `npm install`.
2. Only run `npm run android:prebuild` when you intentionally want to regenerate the native Android project. It uses `expo prebuild --clean` and will overwrite local native edits.
3. On Windows, prefer `npm run android:studio`. It loads `.env.local`, `.env.staging`, or `.env.production`, writes `android/local.properties`, applies a short `GRADLE_USER_HOME`, and opens the tracked native project with those environment variables.
4. If you open Android Studio manually, create `android/local.properties` locally with:

```properties
sdk.dir=C\:/Users/T/AppData/Local/Android/Sdk
EXPO_PUBLIC_ENV=local
ANDROID_GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY=your-maps-key
ANDROID_ADMOB_APP_ID=ca-app-pub-3940256099942544~3347511713
```

5. Open `android/` in Android Studio and wait for Gradle sync.
6. Start Metro with `npm run start`.
   This wrapper normalizes malformed boolean env vars such as `CI=1 ` that would otherwise crash Expo CLI before Metro starts.
7. Run the `app` configuration from Android Studio, or build from the terminal with `npm run android:assembleDebug`.
8. For a faster Windows emulator-only smoke build, use `npm run android:assembleDebug:emulator`.

## AdMob notes

- Set `ANDROID_ADMOB_APP_ID` and `EXPO_PUBLIC_ADMOB_CODE_REVEAL_UNIT_ID` in the active env file for rewarded code reveals.
- Local and staging Android builds fall back to Google's test app id when `ANDROID_ADMOB_APP_ID` is blank.
- Production builds should use your real AdMob app id and rewarded unit id.

## Release workflow

- Local Android builds: Android Studio or `npm run android:assembleDebug`
- Windows Android Studio launch helper: `npm run android:studio`
- Fast Windows emulator verification: `npm run android:assembleDebug:emulator`
- Android release artifacts: EAS via `eas.json`
- iOS local builds: `npx expo run:ios`

# Pee-Dom Launch Checklist

Target: Top 100 Utility on iOS App Store & Google Play

---

## 1. App Store Assets

### iOS (App Store Connect)
- [ ] App name: "Pee-Dom: Find Bathrooms Fast"
- [ ] Subtitle (30 chars): "Crowd-Sourced Restroom Finder"
- [ ] Keywords (100 chars): bathroom,restroom,finder,nearby,accessible,code,public,emergency,clean,toilet
- [ ] App icon (1024x1024 PNG, no alpha, no rounded corners)
- [ ] Screenshots: 6.7" (iPhone 15 Pro Max), 6.5" (iPhone 11 Pro Max), 5.5" (iPhone 8 Plus)
  - [ ] Map view with pins
  - [ ] Emergency "Go Now" button in action
  - [ ] Bathroom detail with code + cleanliness rating
  - [ ] Search with filters
  - [ ] Accessibility features
  - [ ] Gamification profile with badges
- [ ] iPad screenshots (if universal): 12.9" (6th gen)
- [ ] App preview video (15-30 sec, optional but recommended)
- [ ] Privacy policy URL hosted and live
- [ ] Support URL live
- [ ] Age rating questionnaire completed
- [ ] App category: Utilities (primary), Navigation (secondary)

### Android (Google Play Console)
- [ ] App icon (512x512 PNG)
- [ ] Feature graphic (1024x500 PNG)
- [ ] Screenshots: phone (min 2), 7" tablet, 10" tablet
- [ ] Short description (80 chars): "Find the nearest bathroom instantly. Crowd-sourced codes & ratings."
- [ ] Full description (4000 chars max)
- [ ] Content rating questionnaire
- [ ] Data safety form completed
- [ ] Target API level >= 34 (Android 14)
- [ ] Privacy policy URL

---

## 2. Environment Variable Security

### Required Production Variables
- [ ] `EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL` - Production Supabase project URL
- [ ] `EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY` - Production anon key (NOT service role key)
- [ ] `EXPO_PUBLIC_SENTRY_DSN` - Production Sentry project DSN
- [ ] `EXPO_PUBLIC_ENV=production` - Environment flag

### Security Audit
- [ ] `.env` is listed in `.gitignore` (verified)
- [ ] `.env.example` has placeholder values only (verified)
- [ ] No service role keys in client code
- [ ] No API keys hardcoded in source (grep for patterns: `sk_`, `key_`, `secret`)
- [ ] `expo-secure-store` used for any sensitive user credentials
- [ ] Supabase RLS enabled on ALL tables (19 migrations verified)
- [ ] `assert_active_user()` guard on all mutation RPCs (migration 019 verified)
- [ ] AdMob app IDs switch to production values in `app.config.ts`
- [ ] Google Maps API key restricted to app bundle ID
- [ ] Sentry source maps uploaded but not publicly accessible

### EAS Build Secrets
- [ ] All production env vars set in EAS Secrets (not in eas.json)
- [ ] `eas.json` production profile uses `distribution: "store"`
- [ ] iOS provisioning profile + distribution certificate configured
- [ ] Android keystore configured for production signing

---

## 3. Error Logging & Monitoring

### Sentry Configuration
- [ ] `@sentry/react-native` initialized in `_layout.tsx` (verified)
- [ ] Production DSN is NOT the development DSN
- [ ] `tracesSampleRate` set to 0.1 for production (verified in `config.ts`)
- [ ] User context attached (ID, role, premium status)
- [ ] React Query errors forwarded to Sentry (verified in `query-client.ts`)
- [ ] Source maps uploaded via `@sentry/expo` plugin in EAS build
- [ ] Alert rules configured for: crash rate > 1%, new issue spike
- [ ] Release health tracking enabled

### Analytics
- [ ] Analytics endpoint configured for production
- [ ] `app_bootstrapped` event tracking verified
- [ ] `emergency_mode_activated` event tracking verified
- [ ] Key funnel events tracked: search, favorite, code_submit, code_reveal, report

---

## 4. Performance Benchmarks

### Startup
- [ ] Cold start to interactive map < 3 seconds on mid-range device
- [ ] Splash screen covers full bootstrap (fonts + auth + initial location)
- [ ] Offline launch shows cached data within 1 second

### Map
- [ ] Map renders 150 pins without jank (clustering enabled via react-native-map-clustering)
- [ ] Region change debounced at 300ms (verified in `useBathrooms.ts`)
- [ ] Emergency mode locates + navigates in < 5 seconds

### Network
- [ ] Supabase requests timeout at 15s (resilientFetch verified)
- [ ] Offline queue processes on reconnect (useOfflineSync verified)
- [ ] Realtime reconnects with exponential backoff (verified)
- [ ] React Query stale time: 5 min, GC time: 10 min (verified)

---

## 5. Offline & Edge Cases

- [ ] App launches without network and shows cached bathrooms
- [ ] Offline banner displayed when connection lost
- [ ] Mutations queue when offline and replay on reconnect
- [ ] Location permission denied shows graceful fallback
- [ ] Emergency mode handles no-location with clear user message
- [ ] Empty states for: no search results, no favorites, no bathrooms nearby
- [ ] Deactivated account detected and user signed out
- [ ] Deep links to bathroom detail handle deleted bathrooms

---

## 6. Accessibility Compliance

- [ ] All buttons have `accessibilityRole="button"` and `accessibilityLabel`
- [ ] Emergency button has descriptive accessibility label (verified)
- [ ] Screen reader announces map pin selection
- [ ] Contrast ratios meet WCAG AA (brand-600 on white, ink-900 on surface-base)
- [ ] Touch targets >= 44x44 points (emergency button: 64x64 verified)
- [ ] Accessibility mode filters work end-to-end
- [ ] VoiceOver (iOS) and TalkBack (Android) manual testing completed

---

## 7. Legal & Compliance

- [ ] Privacy policy covers: location data, email, push tokens, analytics
- [ ] Terms of service published
- [ ] GDPR: account deactivation deletes push subscriptions (verified in migration 017)
- [ ] CCPA: no personal data sold to third parties
- [ ] COPPA: age gate if app targets under 13 (not applicable for PeeDom)
- [ ] AdMob GDPR consent (UMP SDK) integrated for EU users
- [ ] Location usage description strings set in `app.config.ts`:
  - iOS: `NSLocationWhenInUseUsageDescription`
  - Android: `ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION` + `ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATION`

---

## 8. Pre-Submission Testing

### Device Matrix
- [ ] iPhone SE (3rd gen) - smallest screen
- [ ] iPhone 15 Pro - primary iOS target
- [ ] iPhone 15 Pro Max - largest screen
- [ ] Pixel 7a - mid-range Android
- [ ] Samsung Galaxy S24 - flagship Android
- [ ] Android Go device (1-2 GB RAM) - stress test

### Test Scenarios
- [ ] Fresh install â†’ onboarding â†’ map loads
- [ ] Sign up â†’ submit bathroom â†’ earn points â†’ see badge
- [ ] Emergency "Go Now" â†’ navigation launches
- [ ] Offline â†’ queue mutations â†’ reconnect â†’ mutations process
- [ ] Background â†’ foreground â†’ realtime channels reconnect
- [ ] Search â†’ filter â†’ select result â†’ view detail â†’ favorite
- [ ] Code submit â†’ code vote â†’ code reveal via rewarded ad
- [ ] Business claim â†’ verification badge appears
- [ ] Push notification received â†’ tap â†’ navigates to bathroom detail
- [ ] Rotate device â†’ layout remains correct

---

## 9. App Store Review Preparation

### Common Rejection Reasons to Preempt
- [ ] Login is optional (guest browsing works)
- [ ] Location permission requested in context (not on first launch)
- [ ] Ad frequency is reasonable (rewarded ads only, user-initiated)
- [ ] No placeholder content in screenshots
- [ ] App description matches actual functionality
- [ ] In-app purchases (if any) described accurately

### Demo Account
- [ ] Create test account credentials for Apple reviewer
- [ ] Pre-populate account with: favorites, badges, submitted codes
- [ ] Document in App Store review notes

---

## 10. Launch Day

- [ ] Production Supabase database seeded with initial bathroom data
- [ ] Sentry alerts configured and on-call rotation set
- [ ] EAS production build submitted to both stores
- [ ] App Store review initiated (allow 24-48 hrs)
- [ ] Social media launch post prepared
- [ ] App Store Optimization: first-week keyword monitoring
- [ ] Crash-free rate target: > 99.5% in first 48 hours

| [src/lib/return-intent.ts](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/src/lib/return-intent.ts) | Intentionally absent. Guest-intent replay now lives in memory inside AuthContext and must stay there. |

# **2. Verification Snapshot**

Verified on March 12, 2026 from this workspace.

Local shell note: this PowerShell environment blocks `npm.ps1` and `npx.ps1`, so validation was run with `npm.cmd` and `npx.cmd`.

| Command | Result |
| :---- | :---- |
| `npm.cmd run type-check` | PASS |
| `npm.cmd run lint` | PASS |
| `npm.cmd test -- --runInBand` | PASS |
| `npx.cmd expo config --type public` | PASS |
| `npm.cmd run android:prebuild` | PASS |
| `npm.cmd run android:assembleDebug:emulator` | PASS |

## **2.1 Test Coverage Present**

| Test File | Status |
| :---- | :---- |
| [__tests__/validate.test.ts](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/__tests__/validate.test.ts) | Passing |
| [__tests__/navigation.test.ts](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/__tests__/navigation.test.ts) | Passing |
| [__tests__/queued-mutations.test.ts](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/__tests__/queued-mutations.test.ts) | Passing |
| [__tests__/bathroom-utils.test.ts](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/__tests__/bathroom-utils.test.ts) | Passing |
| [__tests__/runtime-guards.test.ts](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/__tests__/runtime-guards.test.ts) | Passing |

# **3. Active Blockers**

## **3.1 Dependency Drift**

`npx.cmd expo install --check` failed on March 11, 2026. In this shell it also reported that networking was disabled and validation was using Expo's local dependency map, so the output is directionally useful but not perfect.

The key point is that this is not just a "missing packages" problem anymore. The reported drift now includes core-stack expectations such as:

| Package | Installed | Expected by check |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| `react` | `18.3.1` | `19.1.0` |
| `react-native` | `0.76.5` | `0.81.5` |
| `expo-router` | `~4.0.11` | `~6.0.23` |
| `react-native-reanimated` | `~3.16.1` | `~4.1.1` |
| `react-native-safe-area-context` | `4.12.0` | `~5.6.0` |
| `react-native-screens` | `~4.3.0` | `~4.16.0` |

Recommendation:

1. Treat dependency alignment as a separate upgrade track, not as a simple pre-Phase-4 package-install task.
2. Do not blindly run `expo install` across the whole drift list under the current pinned architecture.
3. Decide explicitly whether PeeDom is staying on the current validated stack or upgrading to the newer Expo 54 ecosystem that `--check` expects.

## **3.2 Local Android Build Environment**

The local Android Studio workflow is now configured and verified on this machine.

Current state on March 12, 2026:

| Check | Result |
| :---- | :---- |
| Android SDK default path | Present at `C:\Users\T\AppData\Local\Android\Sdk` |
| Java runtime | Android Studio JBR configured for the user environment at `C:\Program Files\Android\Android Studio\jbr` |
| `android/local.properties` | Present locally and ignored by Git |
| Expo Android prebuild | PASS via `npm.cmd run android:prebuild` |
| Debug APK build | PASS via `npm.cmd run android:assembleDebug:emulator` |
| Debug artifact | Generated at `android/app/build/outputs/apk/debug/app-debug.apk` |

Operational note:

The full multi-ABI `assembleDebug` build is significantly slower on this Windows machine than the shell timeout used in this workspace. The supported smoke-check path is the emulator-focused `x86_64` build, while Android Studio can still build the selected target ABI during normal local development.

## **3.3 Release Automation**

The repo now has baseline GitHub mobile verification CI in [.github/workflows/mobile-verify.yml](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/.github/workflows/mobile-verify.yml).

What still remains for full mobile CI/CD:

1. Main branch authenticated `eas build`
2. Release-tag authenticated `eas build` plus `eas submit`
3. Expo GitHub App / `EXPO_TOKEN` / project linkage for cloud automation

## **3.4 Device Verification Gap**

The repo implementation exists for map, location, search, favorites, and offline sync, but it still lacks verified device-level confirmation for:

1. Android location permission flow
2. Android map rendering
3. Offline favorite replay on a real reconnect event
4. iOS parity for location and map behavior

# **4. Architecture Contracts**

## **4.1 The Three Laws**

1. Pre-Flight: simulate the dependency graph before writing. Verify imports, downstream effects, and data contracts.
2. Native Purity: banned web primitives stay banned. Use `View`, `Text`, `AsyncStorage`, `Alert.alert()`, and navigation helpers.
3. Atomic: edit whole files only. No placeholder snippets.

## **4.2 Auth Contracts**

| Contract | Rule |
| :---- | :---- |
| Single client | Only [src/lib/supabase.ts](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/src/lib/supabase.ts) may create the client. |
| States | `BOOTSTRAPPING`, `GUEST`, `SESSION_RECOVERY`, `AUTHENTICATED_USER`, `AUTHENTICATED_BUSINESS`, `AUTHENTICATED_ADMIN`, `SESSION_INVALID` |
| Splash | Splash may hide only after the app exits `BOOTSTRAPPING`. |
| `refreshUser()` | Must demote to `GUEST` on any failure. |
| Guest intent replay | In-memory only through AuthContext. Do not persist it. |
| Direct redirects | Use navigation helpers, not ad hoc route string handling. |

## **4.3 Offline Contracts**

| Contract | Rule |
| :---- | :---- |
| Queue eligibility | Authenticated transient failures only |
| Persistence | Zod-validated on load |
| Scope | User-scoped |
| Retry cap | 3 retries max |
| Sleep / backoff | No in-loop sleep; replay is event-driven |
| Mutation outcome | `completed | auth_required | queued_retry` |

## **4.4 Supabase / RLS Contracts**

| Contract | Rule |
| :---- | :---- |
| Public bathroom reads | Must respect public views and RLS |
| `profiles.role` | Immutable from client; guarded in [002_functions.sql](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/supabase/migrations/002_functions.sql) |
| Client secrets | Never use service-role keys in client code |
| Auth replay storage | Do not recreate persisted return-intent storage |

# **5. Phase Status**

## **5.1 Completed**

| Phase | Status |
| :---- | :---- |
| Phase 1 | Complete |
| Phase 2 | Complete |
| Phase 3 | Complete |

## **5.2 Current Reality**

Phase 4 is not "not started". The repo already contains the core Phase 4 implementation for map, search, favorites, and location.

What is true as of March 11, 2026:

1. The major Phase 4 screens and supporting hooks/components exist in code.
2. The repo passes `npm.cmd run check-deps`, `npx.cmd expo config --type public`, `npm.cmd run lint`, `npm.cmd run type-check`, and `npm.cmd test`.
3. Local Android native build verification now passes through the tracked Android Studio surface with `npm.cmd run android:prebuild` and `npm.cmd run android:assembleDebug:emulator`.
4. Dependency drift is resolved in this workspace after aligning the Expo SDK 54 companion package set and declaring Zustand explicitly.
5. Baseline GitHub mobile verification CI now exists; authenticated EAS build and submit automation is still pending.

## **5.3 Do Not Reintroduce Old Assumptions**

The following old assumptions are now false and must not be copied back into status blocks:

1. "Phase 4 not started"
2. "Map/search/favorites are still scaffolds"
3. "Required packages are missing"
4. "Placeholder assets are missing"
5. "return-intent.ts is the replay mechanism"

# **6. Remaining Delivery Work**

## **6.1 Near-Term Safe Work**

These are safe to do next without forcing a stack upgrade:

1. Add authenticated EAS build and submit automation once Expo GitHub App / `EXPO_TOKEN` / project linkage are configured
2. Add screen-level integration coverage for auth replay and offline sync
3. Verify [002_functions.sql](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/supabase/migrations/002_functions.sql) is applied in the target Supabase project
4. Device-test the existing Phase 4 flows now that Android local tooling is available again
5. Replace temporary app art with final branded assets before store submission

## **6.2 Work That Needs Explicit Approval**

These are not safe "background fixes" and should be treated as deliberate tracks:

1. Additional Expo / React / React Native / Expo Router upgrades beyond the currently aligned stack
2. Any further dependency realignment that changes generated native code or routing behavior
3. Any architectural rewrite of AuthContext beyond targeted cleanup

## **6.3 Release Gaps Still Open**

| Area | Status |
| :---- | :---- |
| Android local build | Verified locally through Android Studio / emulator ABI debug build |
| Device validation | Pending |
| CI/CD | Baseline GitHub verification added; authenticated EAS build and submit automation pending |
| Store metadata | Pending |
| Final art | Pending |
| Screen integration tests | Pending |

# **7. Builder Directive**

Use this exact block for future coding sessions:

SYSTEM: ACTIVATE [BUILDER_PRIME_MODE]
Project: Pee-Dom (bathroom-finder, iOS + Android)
Repo: Tdub206/Pee-Dom
Stack: React 19.1.0, React Native 0.81.5, Expo SDK 54, Expo Router v6, TypeScript strict, NativeWind v4, Supabase (PostgreSQL + PostGIS + Auth), TanStack Query v5, AsyncStorage
STATUS:
- Phase 1/2/3: complete.
- Phase 4 repo implementation exists for map, search, favorites, and location.
- March 11, 2026 verification in this workspace: `npm.cmd run check-deps`, `npx.cmd expo config --type public`, `npm.cmd run lint`, `npm.cmd run type-check`, and `npm.cmd test` all pass.
- Active blockers: local Android SDK/adb missing, device-level verification still pending, authenticated EAS build or submit automation pending, final store metadata and art still pending.
THE 3 LAWS:
1. Pre-Flight: simulate the full dependency graph before writing.
2. Native Purity: banned web primitives stay banned. Use React Native primitives and `Alert.alert()`.
3. Atomic: output complete files only.
KEY CONTRACTS:
- Single Supabase client only in `src/lib/supabase.ts`
- `refreshUser()` demotes to `GUEST` on any failure
- Guest-intent replay is in-memory only via AuthContext
- Offline queue is authenticated-only, Zod-validated, user-scoped, max 3 retries
- `MutationOutcome` is `completed | auth_required | queued_retry`
- `profiles.role` is immutable from client
- Splash hides only after exiting `BOOTSTRAPPING`
- This manifest is canonical

name: EAS Release

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      platform:
        description: Which platform to build
        required: true
        default: all
        type: choice
        options:
          - all
          - android
          - ios
      profile:
        description: Which EAS build profile to use
        required: true
        default: preview
        type: choice
        options:
          - preview
          - production
      auto_submit:
        description: Submit to the configured store after the build completes
        required: true
        default: false
        type: boolean
  pull_request:
    branches:
      - main
  push:
    branches:
      - main
    tags:
      - 'v*'

concurrency:
  group: eas-release-${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: false

jobs:
  release:
    if: github.event_name != 'pull_request' || github.event.pull_request.head.repo.fork == false
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
    env:
      EXPO_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.EXPO_TOKEN }}
      EAS_PROJECT_ID: ${{ secrets.EAS_PROJECT_ID }}
      EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL: ${{ secrets.EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL }}
      EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY: ${{ secrets.EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY }}
      EXPO_PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL: ${{ vars.EXPO_PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL }}
      EXPO_PUBLIC_SENTRY_DSN: ${{ secrets.EXPO_PUBLIC_SENTRY_DSN }}
      EXPO_PUBLIC_ENV: ${{ github.event_name == 'pull_request' && 'preview' || github.event_name == 'push' && 'production' || inputs.profile }}
      ANDROID_GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANDROID_GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY }}
      IOS_GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.IOS_GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY }}
    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - name: Setup Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v6
        with:
          node-version: 22
          cache: npm

      - name: Setup Expo and EAS CLI
        uses: expo/expo-github-action@v8
        with:
          expo-version: latest
          eas-version: latest
          token: ${{ secrets.EXPO_TOKEN }}

      - name: Validate release secrets
        shell: bash
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          missing=0
          required_vars=("EXPO_TOKEN" "EAS_PROJECT_ID" "EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL" "EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY" "EXPO_PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL")
          for var in "${required_vars[@]}"; do
            if [[ -z "${!var}" ]]; then
              echo "::error::$var is required for EAS releases."
              missing=1
            fi
          done
          exit "$missing"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: Verify Expo dependency alignment
        run: npm run check-deps

      - name: Verify Expo config
        run: npx expo config --type public > /dev/null

      - name: Lint
        run: npm run lint

      - name: Type check
        run: npm run type-check

      - name: Run tests
        run: npm test -- --runInBand

      - name: Resolve release arguments
        id: release_args
        shell: bash
        run: |
          if [[ "${GITHUB_REF}" == refs/tags/* ]]; then
            echo "platform=all" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
            echo "profile=production" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
            echo "auto_submit=true" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          elif [[ "${GITHUB_EVENT_NAME}" == "push" && "${GITHUB_REF}" == "refs/heads/main" ]]; then
            echo "platform=all" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
            echo "profile=production" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
            echo "auto_submit=false" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          elif [[ "${GITHUB_EVENT_NAME}" == "pull_request" ]]; then
            echo "platform=all" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
            echo "profile=preview" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
            echo "auto_submit=false" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          else
            echo "platform=${{ inputs.platform }}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
            echo "profile=${{ inputs.profile }}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
            echo "auto_submit=${{ inputs.auto_submit }}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          fi

      - name: Build with EAS
        shell: bash
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          COMMAND=(eas build --platform "${{ steps.release_args.outputs.platform }}" --profile "${{ steps.release_args.outputs.profile }}" --non-interactive)
          if [[ "${{ steps.release_args.outputs.auto_submit }}" == "true" ]]; then
            COMMAND+=(--auto-submit)
          fi
          "${COMMAND[@]}"

payload.message: The release workflow exists, and AdMob consent is actually implemented better than I expected. I’m doing one last feature-surface read of the map/search screens so the “what’s undercooked vs what’s next-level” part is grounded in the shipped product, not generic app advice.

content.text: The release workflow exists, and AdMob consent is actually implemented better than I expected. I’m doing one last feature-surface read of the map/search screens so the “what’s undercooked vs what’s next-level” part is grounded in the shipped product, not generic app advice.

is this branch the most current branch compared to my local files? can you do a complete code review of my entire codebase and project and fix any errors you see or anything that would cause android studio to break specifically but not limited to relating to any Hermes scripts

**Findings**
- No. `feature/full-build` matches `Codex/feature/full-build` at `df483bf` on March 22, 2026, but it is not the most current integrated branch. It does not contain `master` (`69547c0`), the Hermes fix branch (`5f20c47`), or the Sentry fix branch (`5d85db9`). Your local worktree is also dirty, so the branch commit and local files are already diverged.
- `[P1]` Hermes-safe catch bindings were missing in 11 live app/source files, which is exactly the kind of syntax that can break Hermes on Android. I fixed those in places like [favorites.tsx](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/app/(tabs)/favorites.tsx#L54) and [CodeRevealCard.tsx](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/src/components/CodeRevealCard.tsx#L57).
- `[P1]` TypeScript and Jest were validating archived code outside the active PeeDom app, which caused false failures from `.claude/worktrees` and `StallPass/`. I fixed the scope in [tsconfig.json](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/tsconfig.json#L33) and [jest.config.js](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/jest.config.js#L7).
- `[P1]` `expo-clipboard` was imported by [CodeRevealCard.tsx](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/src/components/CodeRevealCard.tsx#L3) but missing from `node_modules`. I restored the installed package.
- `[P2]` Expo was warning about ignored AdMob config. I removed the stale root-level config path and left the plugin-driven config as the source of truth in [app.config.ts](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/app.config.ts#L49) and [app.json](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/app.json#L1).
- `[P3]` ESLint was failing on an unused mock constructor parameter in [bathroom-submissions-api.test.ts](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/__tests__/bathroom-submissions-api.test.ts#L8). Fixed.

**Verification**
- `npm run lint` passed.
- `npm run type-check` passed.
- `npm test -- --runInBand` passed: 41 suites, 190 tests.
- Android checks passed:
  - `:app:processDebugMainManifest`
  - `:app:createBundleReleaseJsAndAssets`
  - `:app:compileDebugKotlin`
  - `:app:assembleDebug`

**Assumptions / residual**
- I assumed `.claude/worktrees/*` and `StallPass/` are not part of the shipping PeeDom app. If they are intended build targets, the TS/Jest exclusions should be revisited.
- Android Studio should sync and build now. Remaining warnings are non-blocking: `react-native-google-mobile-ads` emits a warning that explicitly says it is safe when using the Expo config plugin, and Gradle still reports upstream deprecations for future Gradle 9 compatibility.
- I preserved pre-existing dirty Android files in your worktree rather than reverting them.
how do you mean dirty android files?

“Dirty” just means Git sees local, uncommitted changes. It does not mean “bad.”

Right now the Android side is dirty in two ways:

- Real content changes:
  - [AndroidManifest.xml](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/android/app/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml) has two local removals: a hardcoded Google Maps API key and `org.apache.http.legacy`.
  - [gradle.properties](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/android/gradle.properties) has a local addition: `org.gradle.vfs.watch=false`.
  - [gradle-wrapper.properties](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/android/gradle/wrapper/gradle-wrapper.properties) has local wrapper zip-store fixes.

- Line-ending-only noise:
  - [build.gradle](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/android/app/build.gradle)
  - [MainActivity.kt](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/android/app/src/main/java/com/peedom/mobile/MainActivity.kt)
  - [MainApplication.kt](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/android/app/src/main/java/com/peedom/mobile/MainApplication.kt)
  - [strings.xml](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/android/app/src/main/res/values/strings.xml)
  - [settings.gradle](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/android/settings.gradle)

Those five show up because `core.autocrlf=true` on this machine, so Git is noticing LF/CRLF normalization rather than actual code edits.

So when I said “dirty Android files,” I meant “there are local uncommitted Android-path changes in the working tree,” not “the Android files are broken.” If you want, I can next separate them into:
1. real Android changes worth keeping
2. line-ending churn worth normalizing or discarding
so what command am i running from my PeeDom folder to get android studio up and running

From `C:\Users\T\Desktop\PeeDom`, run:

```powershell
npm run android:studio
```

That is the repo’s wrapper for opening Android Studio with the project’s Android env prepped.

Equivalent direct command:

```powershell
node scripts/android-tooling.cjs studio
```

If you want to build from the terminal after Studio opens, use:

```powershell
npm run android:assembleDebug
```
build failed immediately for this C:\Program Files\Android\Android Studio\wrapper\dists\gradle-8.14.3-bin\cv11ve7ro1n3o1j4so8xd9n66\gradle-8.14.3-bin.zip.lck

That `.zip.lck` is a stale Gradle wrapper lock, not a PeeDom compile error. I checked it: there’s a matching `.zip.ok` next to it, which means the Gradle zip finished downloading and the lock file was just left behind. I also verified `:app:assembleDebug` and the Hermes JS bundle succeed from the terminal, so the project itself is building.

Run this from PowerShell:

```powershell
Stop-Process -Name studio64 -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
Remove-Item "C:\Program Files\Android\Android Studio\wrapper\dists\gradle-8.14.3-bin\cv11ve7ro1n3o1j4so8xd9n66\gradle-8.14.3-bin.zip.lck" -Force
cd C:\Users\T\Desktop\PeeDom
npm run android:studio
```

If that `Remove-Item` says access denied, use an elevated PowerShell once and run:

```powershell
Remove-Item "C:\Program Files\Android\Android Studio\wrapper\dists\gradle-8.14.3-bin\cv11ve7ro1n3o1j4so8xd9n66" -Recurse -Force
```

Then relaunch with:

```powershell
cd C:\Users\T\Desktop\PeeDom
npm run android:studio
```

If Studio still tries to use `C:\Program Files\Android\Android Studio\wrapper\dists\...`, set Gradle user home inside Studio to:

```text
C:\Users\T\.g\peedom
```

Path:
`Settings > Build, Execution, Deployment > Build Tools > Gradle > Gradle user home`

If you want, I can also patch the launcher script to be more aggressive about cleaning stale Studio wrapper locks.

<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
  - Archiving rule: only include `::archive-thread{}` when there is nothing actionable for the user.
  - Safe to archive: "no findings" checks (bug scans that found nothing, clean lint runs, monitoring checks with no incidents).
  - Do not archive: deliverables or follow-ups (briefs, reports, summaries, plans, recommendations).
  - If you do archive, include the archive directive after the inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.
</app-context>

<collaboration_mode># Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.
</collaboration_mode>

# Pee-Dom Agent Operating Contract

This repository is a mobile-first application named Pee-Dom.

## Product Scope
Pee-Dom is a restroom-finder mobile application for iOS and Android users. It is built for launch readiness, reliability, offline tolerance, and app-store compliant mobile UX.

## Platform Priorities
1. Mobile-first behavior
2. Expo / React Native compatibility
3. TypeScript correctness
4. Supabase schema and RLS correctness
5. Offline-first behavior
6. Safe-area, navigation, permissions, and degraded-network handling
7. Production debugging, telemetry readiness, and launch hardening

## Engineering Constraints
- Prefer React Native primitives, Expo-compatible solutions, and TypeScript-safe patterns
- Do not introduce web-only assumptions into mobile flows
- Protect auth/session, location access, and user-submitted data
- Any change touching data flow must consider:
  - offline queue behavior
  - optimistic updates
  - retry semantics
  - idempotency
  - RLS implications
- Any change touching navigation must consider:
  - Expo Router route behavior
  - back-stack behavior
  - deep-link viability
  - modal interaction
- Any change touching maps/location must consider:
  - permission denial states
  - stale location
  - background/foreground transitions
  - battery and performance costs

## Review Standard
All agent reviews should evaluate:
- correctness
- edge cases
- race conditions
- offline behavior
- mobile UX regressions
- security/privacy issues
- Supabase/RLS impact
- launch risk

## Output Standard
Agents must produce:
- explicit assumptions
- concrete file targets
- exact code changes or diffs
- risks
- test checklist
</INSTRUCTIONS>

<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
  - Archiving rule: only include `::archive-thread{}` when there is nothing actionable for the user.
  - Safe to archive: "no findings" checks (bug scans that found nothing, clean lint runs, monitoring checks with no incidents).
  - Do not archive: deliverables or follow-ups (briefs, reports, summaries, plans, recommendations).
  - If you do archive, include the archive directive after the inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.
</app-context>

# PeeDom Core Architect

Operate as the PeeDom lead engineer. Verify the live repository architecture first, then implement only within the patterns that actually exist. Treat "Pee-Dom Final" as the current checked-in architecture, not a presumed native Android template.

## First pass

1. Read the repository instructions first: local `AGENTS.md`, `package.json`, `app.config.ts`, `app/_layout.tsx`, `tailwind.config.js`, `src/lib/supabase.ts`, `src/lib/query-client.ts`, and the relevant files under `src/`, `app/`, `android/`, and `__tests__/`.
2. Verify the active surface before writing code:
   - Expo Router navigation lives in `app/`.
   - TypeScript business logic lives in `src/`.
   - NativeWind styling is configured in `tailwind.config.js`.
   - React Query is the server-state layer.
   - Zustand is the client-state layer.
   - Supabase access must go through the typed client and RLS-safe queries.
   - `android/` is a generated native surface that must stay aligned with Expo config.
3. Decide whether the request belongs in:
   - Expo TypeScript only
   - Expo config plus Android verification
   - Direct Android source changes that cannot be avoided
4. Prefer the Expo source of truth. For permissions, plugins, package config, deep links, versioning, or ads setup, update `app.config.ts` first and then verify the generated Android files still match.

## Hard constraints

- PeeDom style only. Reuse the repository's naming, route structure, hooks, store layout, and `@/` import aliases.
- No generic Android answers. If the request assumes Hilt, Koin, Navigation Component, Compose, XML screens, feature modules, or `libs.versions.toml`, confirm those files exist before using them.
- Do not invent native package paths such as `com.peedom.app.ui.features...` when the repository actually uses Expo routes and `src/` modules.
- If PeeDom's analytics, networking, persistence, ads, or feature flag implementation is unclear, ask for the exact file only when the answer cannot be derived from the repository.
- Dependency locking is strict. Use only the libraries and versions already present in `package.json`, `app.config.ts` plugins, or the checked-in Android Gradle files unless the user explicitly requests dependency work.

## Implementation workflow

1. Map the request onto the existing PeeDom layers:
   - UI and navigation: `app/` and `src/components/`
   - Feature hooks and orchestration: `src/hooks/`
   - API access: `src/api/`
   - Shared runtime utilities: `src/lib/`
   - Client state: `src/store/`
   - Types and validation helpers: `src/types/` and `src/utils/`
2. Preserve PeeDom's state model:
   - React Query for network-backed server state
   - Zustand for app-local UI state
   - Local component state only for truly local concerns
3. Every async path must include explicit loading, failure handling, and a user-friendly recovery or empty-state path.
4. Use strict TypeScript. Avoid `any`. Extend the existing domain types instead of weakening them.
5. Validate new user inputs and external payloads with Zod when the repository already validates that surface or when a new untrusted payload is introduced.
6. When Android-specific hooks are required, cross-check `app.config.ts`, `android/app/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml`, `android/build.gradle`, and `android/app/build.gradle`.
7. When direct Android edits are unavoidable, keep them minimal and consistent with Expo prebuild expectations. Call out when a future prebuild could overwrite the change.
8. Provide complete code and exact real file paths from this repository. Never return placeholders, stubs, or pseudo-implementations.

## Architecture guardrails

- Prevent circular dependencies across `app/`, `src/api/`, `src/hooks/`, `src/lib/`, `src/store/`, `src/contexts/`, and `src/components/`.
- Move pure mapping, parsing, and cache-key logic into `src/utils/`, `src/lib/`, or `src/types/` when that reduces coupling.
- Keep Supabase access typed and centralized. Do not bypass the existing runtime config or use privileged keys.
- Preserve Android and iOS parity for navigation, safe areas, keyboard behavior, and permission messaging unless the task is intentionally platform-specific.
- Keep NativeWind usage aligned with the central theme instead of scattering one-off style systems.

## Refactoring and cleanup

- During feature work, call out and fix nearby technical debt when it materially improves correctness, modularity, or release safety.
- Prefer small, production-safe refactors over broad rewrites.
- If the repository surface contradicts the user's assumption, state the verified architecture and continue with the production-safe implementation for that architecture.

## Verification

1. Add or update a corresponding test under `__tests__/` for any new user-visible logic or behavior.
2. After modifying logic, run `npm run lint` and `npm test`.
3. Run additional verification when the change touches the Android surface, such as the repo's Android build scripts, if practical.
4. If any validation cannot run, report exactly what is still unverified.

## Response style

- Be direct and specific.
- State which PeeDom surface was verified before implementation.
- When native Android assumptions do not match the repository, correct the assumption instead of mirroring it.

# Staff Mobile Engineer

Deliver mobile changes with a zero-defect deployment mindset. Identify the active platform surface first, then preserve the repository's stack while enforcing production-grade architecture, validation, testing, and release discipline.

## First pass

1. Read the repository instructions first: local `AGENTS.md`, root configuration, package or build manifests, app configuration, and existing tests.
2. Detect the active surface before proposing changes:
   - Expo or React Native managed app: stay inside Expo Router, TypeScript, NativeWind, Zustand, React Query, Zod, and Supabase generated types.
   - Native Android app or module: use Clean Architecture with Data, Domain, and Presentation layers plus MVVM or MVI, Kotlin Coroutines and Flow, and Jetpack Compose unless legacy XML is explicitly required.
3. Preserve the existing stack. Do not introduce Kotlin or Compose into an Expo or React Native codebase unless the user explicitly requests native Android work or the repository already contains that native surface.
4. Reject placeholders. If a function is called, implement the full production path, including loading, failures, recovery behavior, and edge-case handling.

## Architecture bar

- Design for production scale. Prefer small, composable modules over large screens, hooks, view models, or service objects.
- Separate data, domain, and presentation concerns even in React Native. Keep network and storage logic out of UI components.
- Model state transitions explicitly. Apply MVVM or MVI thinking even when the implementation uses hooks and stores.
- Refactor toward smaller boundaries, lower duplication, and fewer god objects without changing behavior unintentionally.
- Correct architecturally unsound requests directly and replace them with the closest production-safe alternative.

## Expo and React Native rules

- Use TypeScript everywhere. Avoid `any`. Make interfaces and domain types descriptive and exhaustive.
- Use the repo's Expo Router patterns. Keep navigation behavior explicit and predictable.
- Style with NativeWind and the central theme configuration. Promote shared visual tokens instead of scattering one-off values.
- Use Zustand for client-side global state and React Query for server-state caching.
- Validate forms and external payloads with Zod.
- Respect `SafeAreaView`, keyboard offsets, Android back handling, and `Platform.OS` parity concerns.
- Use Supabase with generated types and RLS-safe queries only. Never hardcode service keys or bypass user-scoped access.
- Keep versioning and permissions aligned in `app.json` or `app.config.ts` for Expo-managed apps, then ensure EAS, Fastlane, or GitHub Actions use that source of truth.

## Native Android rules

- Use Kotlin with Coroutines and Flow for asynchronous work.
- Use Jetpack Compose for new UI unless the task is explicitly constrained to legacy XML.
- Keep versioning automated in `build.gradle.kts` or the repository's existing Gradle convention when native Android exists.
- Add or maintain R8 or ProGuard rules when shipping new libraries or reflection-heavy code.
- Use encrypted SharedPreferences or DataStore for sensitive local data.

## Implementation guardrails

- Wrap every async operation in explicit error handling. Provide a loading state and a user-friendly failure path.
- Sanitize user input and validate environment variables before use.
- Never hardcode API keys, secrets, or dummy credentials.
- Optimize re-render behavior in mobile surfaces. Use `React.memo`, `useMemo`, and `useCallback` strategically when component churn is measurable or likely.
- Optimize for low-end devices first. Do not assume flagship hardware, large memory budgets, or perfect network conditions.
- For 120 Hz-sensitive UI, avoid layout thrash, unstable list keys, and heavy synchronous work on interaction paths.

## Testing and verification

- Add or update a corresponding test for every new feature. In this repository, place tests under `__tests__` unless the existing structure clearly dictates otherwise.
- For React Native work, cover domain logic and user-visible flows with the repository's Jest-based setup or the established test harness.
- For native Android work, add both a unit test and a Compose or UI test when that surface exists.
- After modifying logic, run `npm run lint` and `npm test`. Prefer `pnpm` for dependency management, but do not churn lockfiles or package-manager conventions unless asked.
- If lint or tests cannot run, state exactly what blocked validation and what remains unverified.

## Launch readiness

- Keep permissions minimal and justified.
- Ensure versioning, icons, localized strings, and store-facing configuration stay in sync with the release path.
- When asked for deployment automation, generate or update Fastlane or GitHub Actions flows that build release artifacts safely for the current stack: Android App Bundles for native Android, EAS or native release steps for Expo-managed apps, or both when the repository supports both.
- Call out missing adaptive icons, localization coverage, signing inputs, or store metadata before treating the feature as release-ready.

## Response style

- Be concise, direct, and technically exhaustive where it matters.
- Prioritize the production-safe answer over the convenient answer.
- When tradeoffs exist, state the recommendation, the reason, and the operational risk of the alternatives.

name: EAS Release

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      platform:
        description: Which platform to build
        required: true
        default: all
        type: choice
        options:
          - all
          - android
          - ios
      profile:
        description: Which EAS build profile to use
        required: true
        default: preview
        type: choice
        options:
          - preview
          - production
      auto_submit:
        description: Submit to the configured store after the build completes
        required: true
        default: false
        type: boolean
  pull_request:
    branches:
      - main
  push:
    branches:
      - main
    tags:
      - 'v*'

concurrency:
  group: eas-release-${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: false

jobs:
  release:
    if: github.event_name != 'pull_request' || github.event.pull_request.head.repo.fork == false
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
    env:
      EXPO_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.EXPO_TOKEN }}
      EAS_PROJECT_ID: ${{ secrets.EAS_PROJECT_ID }}
      EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL: ${{ secrets.EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL }}
      EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY: ${{ secrets.EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY }}
      EXPO_PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL: ${{ vars.EXPO_PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL }}
      EXPO_PUBLIC_SENTRY_DSN: ${{ secrets.EXPO_PUBLIC_SENTRY_DSN }}
      EXPO_PUBLIC_ENV: ${{ github.event_name == 'pull_request' && 'preview' || github.event_name == 'push' && 'production' || inputs.profile }}
      ANDROID_GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANDROID_GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY }}
      IOS_GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.IOS_GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY }}
    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - name: Setup Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v6
        with:
          node-version: 22
          cache: npm

      - name: Setup Expo and EAS CLI
        uses: expo/expo-github-action@v8
        with:
          expo-version: latest
          eas-version: latest
          token: ${{ secrets.EXPO_TOKEN }}

      - name: Validate release secrets
        shell: bash
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          missing=0
          required_vars=("EXPO_TOKEN" "EAS_PROJECT_ID" "EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL" "EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY" "EXPO_PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL")
          for var in "${required_vars[@]}"; do
            if [[ -z "${!var}" ]]; then
              echo "::error::$var is required for EAS releases."
              missing=1
            fi
          done
          exit "$missing"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: Verify Expo dependency alignment
        run: npm run check-deps

      - name: Verify Expo config
        run: npx expo config --type public > /dev/null

      - name: Lint
        run: npm run lint

      - name: Type check
        run: npm run type-check

      - name: Run tests
        run: npm test -- --runInBand

      - name: Resolve release arguments
        id: release_args
        shell: bash
        run: |
          if [[ "${GITHUB_REF}" == refs/tags/* ]]; then
            echo "platform=all" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
            echo "profile=production" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
            echo "auto_submit=true" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          elif [[ "${GITHUB_EVENT_NAME}" == "push" && "${GITHUB_REF}" == "refs/heads/main" ]]; then
            echo "platform=all" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
            echo "profile=production" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
            echo "auto_submit=false" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          elif [[ "${GITHUB_EVENT_NAME}" == "pull_request" ]]; then
            echo "platform=all" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
            echo "profile=preview" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
            echo "auto_submit=false" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          else
            echo "platform=${{ inputs.platform }}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
            echo "profile=${{ inputs.profile }}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
            echo "auto_submit=${{ inputs.auto_submit }}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          fi

      - name: Build with EAS
        shell: bash
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          COMMAND=(eas build --platform "${{ steps.release_args.outputs.platform }}" --profile "${{ steps.release_args.outputs.profile }}" --non-interactive)
          if [[ "${{ steps.release_args.outputs.auto_submit }}" == "true" ]]; then
            COMMAND+=(--auto-submit)
          fi
          "${COMMAND[@]}"
